coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria gaps and verifiability will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria gaps and verifiability

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1's jq command to assert the correct target ticket id, e.g. `jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .target_id=="<expected-culprit-id>")'`
evidence: The jq filter selects by relation only: `select(.relation=="caused_by")` — no `.target_id` assertion is present
Plan states goal is a link 'to the resolved ticket' but the command doesn't verify which ticket
VCTD #5: missing cardinality/value assertion when the criterion names a specific target
findings.finding: AC1's jq proving command verifies only that SOME `caused_by` link exists on the bug, but does not assert that the link's target_id equals the expected culprit ticket. A pre-existing or spurious caused_by link could make the command exit 0 without the blame resolver having produced the correct result.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")'`
scenarios: A stale caused_by link from a prior test run (shared store) makes AC1 pass with the wrong target
The blame resolver links to the wrong ticket (off-by-one in commit walk) and AC1 still passes
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC1 proving command with: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .target_id=="TICKET-XYZ")'` where TICKET-XYZ is the fixture culprit ticket id.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete absence proving command to AC2, e.g. `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '[.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")]|length == 0'`
evidence: AC2 text contains no jq/shell command, unlike AC1 and AC3 which at least show a command form
Absence assertions require explicit cardinality checks to avoid false-passes (VCTD #5)
AC1 shows a presence jq pattern; AC2 needs the negated equivalent
findings.finding: AC2 (ambiguous two-commit blame yields no auto caused_by link) states 'integration test asserts absence' but provides no concrete proving command in the AC text, leaving the executor to infer what 'asserts absence' means as a deterministic shell check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'an ambiguous two-commit blame yields no auto `caused_by` link — integration test asserts absence'
scenarios: Executor writes a test that passes vacuously (e.g. jq returns empty and the test does not assert exit code)
An unrelated caused_by link is present; the test needs to assert zero caused_by links specifically from the blame path
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '[.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")]|length == 0'` and assert exit code 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the plan whether `--caused-by <id>` validates target ticket existence before writing (and if not, document that as a known limitation with a stated default behavior).
evidence: Plan explicitly states _write_link_event bypasses 'closed-source and cycle guards'
No mention of whether it also bypasses target-existence validation
`add_dependency` at _links.py likely validates target existence; direct-writer may not
findings.finding: The plan does not state what happens when `--caused-by <id>` references a ticket ID that does not exist in the store — _write_link_event is used directly (bypassing add_dependency's existence guards), so a dangling reference could be silently written. No validation, error, or explicit 'write as-is' default is documented.
findings.location: Files — transition_close.py: 'applies the explicit `--caused-by <id>` override … via the `_write_link_event` direct-writer path'
scenarios: User typos the ticket id; a dangling caused_by link is silently written and never surfaced
Downstream provenance metric consumes the dangling edge and crashes on a missing-ticket lookup
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'If `--caused-by <id>` names a non-existent ticket, the write is rejected with a user-visible error (validate via `graph._tickets.exists(id)` before calling `_write_link_event`); or alternatively, document it as caller responsibility with a warning log.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete grep/test command for AC4, e.g. `grep -qF '## Blame-Hunt Advisory' docs/user-guide.md && grep -qF '\-\-caused-by' docs/user-guide.md`
evidence: No grep command, no quoted string anchor, no file path is given in the AC text
'asserted by a test/grep' is a method category, not a concrete proving command (VCTD — no command given)
Without a quoted anchor the executor can satisfy this with any docs change that mentions '--caused-by'
findings.finding: AC4's proving method for the docs advisory ('asserted by a test/grep that the section and the `--caused-by` guidance are present') does not specify the grep pattern, file path, or what constitutes a passing match, making it unmeasurable without executor interpretation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: '`docs/user-guide.md` gains a blame-hunt advisory section … asserted by a test/grep that the section and the `--caused-by` guidance are present'
scenarios: Executor writes a one-line mention of '--caused-by' anywhere in user-guide.md and the grep passes
The section heading differs from '## Blame-Hunt Advisory' in the plan body vs what is actually required — mismatch is undetected
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 proving description with: `grep -qE '^## Blame-Hunt Advisory' docs/user-guide.md && grep -qF '--caused-by' docs/user-guide.md` — both assertions must exit 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to verify that calling `transition()` via the library facade with `caused_by=<id>` produces the same `caused_by` link, covering the `_lib_writes.py` threading path.
evidence: Plan: 'add a `caused_by: str = ""` parameter to `transition()` and thread it into the `transition_compute` call, so library callers (not just the CLI) can set `caused_by` on close'
AC3: '`rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression --caused-by <id>` sets the link regardless of blame' — exercises CLI only, not the library facade
findings.finding: The `_lib_writes.py` deliverable (adding `caused_by: str = ""` to the public `transition()` facade and threading it into `transition_compute`) is described in the Files section but no AC verifies the library-path behavior — AC3 only exercises the CLI (`rebar transition ... --caused-by <id>`), leaving the library facade untested by any stated criterion.
findings.location: Files section (`src/rebar/_lib_writes.py`) vs AC3
scenarios: A library caller passes `caused_by=` and it is silently dropped because the facade threading was omitted or incorrectly wired — no AC would catch this regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 or add AC5: 'Calling `lib_writes.transition(bug_id, "in_progress", "closed", class_="regression", caused_by=<id>)` directly (no CLI) also produces a `caused_by` link — asserted via `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e ...`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `caused_by: str = ""` (or equivalent sentinel) as the explicit default for `close_ticket`'s new parameter, matching the pattern used for `transition_compute`.
evidence: Plan: '`close_ticket` gains a `caused_by` parameter' — no default specified
Contrast: '`transition_compute` gains a `caused_by: str = ""` keyword' — default explicitly given
T4: consumer-facing interface change without stated default leaves backward-compat status unclear
findings.finding: `close_ticket`'s new `caused_by` parameter is described without an explicit default value, unlike `transition_compute` which explicitly specifies `caused_by: str = ""`. This leaves ambiguity about whether existing call sites that omit the argument remain backward-compatible.
findings.location: Files section (`src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`) — `close_ticket` parameter description
scenarios: An existing internal caller of `close_ticket` that omits `caused_by` (e.g., a test helper or a non-bug close path) raises a TypeError at runtime if the parameter lacks a default.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section for `transition_close.py`, change 'close_ticket gains a `caused_by` parameter' to 'close_ticket gains a `caused_by: str = ""` keyword argument (default empty string means no override — matches `transition_compute` pattern).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a persistent design note (ADR entry, AGENTS.md section, or inline doc-block in `_links.py` / `transition_close.py`) explaining WHY `_write_link_event` is called directly for `caused_by` on closed bugs and why the guard bypass is safe for this relation.
evidence: Plan justifies bypass inline: 'bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards — which add nothing for a non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing relation on a closed source'
No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry is listed in the Files section or ACs
This reasoning will not survive as a living artifact warning future maintainers
findings.finding: The direct-writer bypass of `_write_link_event` (skipping the closed-source and cycle guards) is a novel architectural decision with correctness implications, but no persistent design artifact (ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc section) is planned to capture why the bypass is safe and intentional — only inline prose in the plan itself.
findings.location: Files section: transition_close.py bullet; Approach section: 'the edge is written via `_write_link_event` directly'
scenarios: A future maintainer sees `_write_link_event` called directly, treats it as a bug, and 'fixes' it to `add_dependency` — which then raises on closed source, silently dropping the link
A future change adds a required side-effect to `add_dependency` (e.g., audit log) that the bypass skips
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Architecture note` subsection to `docs/user-guide.md` (or a new `docs/adr/caused-by-bypass.md`) documenting: (1) closed-source guard prevents `add_dependency` from closed tickets; (2) `caused_by` on a closed bug is a historical attribution, not a blocking dependency, so the guard adds no value here; (3) `_write_link_event` is the approved escape hatch for this case. Also add a comment block at the call site in `transition_close.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Introduce a named escape-hatch method on the links layer (e.g., `_links.write_attribution_link(source, target, relation, tracker_dir)`) that wraps `_write_link_event` for the closed-source attribution use-case, so the coupling is encapsulated and the bypass is explicit and discoverable rather than a raw private-method call from a command module.
evidence: '_write_link_event' is a private method (underscore-prefixed), not a published interface
No facade, adapter, or wrapper method on the links layer is proposed to encapsulate the bypass
The same private call is used for both auto-derivation and explicit `--caused-by` override
findings.finding: The call to the private method `graph._links._write_link_event` from `transition_close.py` introduces a cross-component coupling on a private API with no interface-level mitigation, so any refactor of `_links.py` internals (rename, signature change, new required arguments) silently breaks auto-derivation with no type-system or API-boundary warning.
findings.location: Files section: transition_close.py bullet — 'writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` directly'
scenarios: `_write_link_event` is renamed or gains a required parameter during a future refactor of `_links.py` — `transition_close.py` breaks at runtime, not at import time
A new required side-effect (e.g., provenance event) is added inside `add_dependency` but not inside `_write_link_event` — caused_by links silently miss the side-effect

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the majority threshold into a named constant (e.g., `BLAME_MAJORITY_THRESHOLD = 0.50`) in `blame.py` and document it (with its rationale) in the blame-hunt advisory section of `docs/user-guide.md`, so it is visible and easily tunable.
evidence: Threshold stated as a fixed design choice: 'strict majority (> 50%)'
No configuration point, constant, or env-var is mentioned to allow tuning
Plan notes 'multi-commit spread ambiguity' as a known failure mode, suggesting threshold sensitivity matters
findings.finding: The >50% majority threshold in `blame.py` is a heuristic that is asserted (not derived from data) and hardcoded, making it non-configurable for projects with different blame distributions (e.g., a codebase with many small files may behave differently than expected).
findings.location: Files section: blame.py bullet — 'strict majority (> 50%) of the blamed lines'
scenarios: A project's file_impact files are large with many small contributors — the 50% bar is never met and auto-derivation never fires, silently degrading to no-link without user awareness
A future team wants to relax to 40% for a high-churn codebase — requires a code change instead of a config update
findings.suggested_fix: Define `BLAME_MAJORITY_THRESHOLD: float = 0.50` at module level in `blame.py`; reference it in the doc advisory so operators know it exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a timeout (e.g., `subprocess.run(..., timeout=10)`) and a top-level `try/except` in `blame.py` so that any git subprocess failure or hang is caught and returns `None`, never blocking the close.
evidence: Plan specifies 'blame the pre-fix state … over the bug's recorded file_impact' and 'the resolver returns a culprit commit ONLY when …' but names no timeout, try/except, or subprocess kill path.
ABSENCE: no mention of timeout, circuit-breaker, or exception wrapping around git subprocess calls.
findings.finding: The `git blame` subprocess (and `git log` scan) invoked in `blame.py` has no specified timeout or exception handler. A slow or hung git process would block the `rebar transition … closed` command indefinitely, degrading an unrelated user action (the close itself).
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — Fixing-commit discovery / Blame target sections
scenarios: Large repo with thousands of commits — git log scan hangs for minutes, freezing the close command.
Network-mounted repo (e.g., NFS) where git blame blocks on I/O.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap all subprocess calls in `blame.py` in `try/except (subprocess.TimeoutExpired, subprocess.CalledProcessError, OSError)` with a configurable timeout (default 10 s), logging a warning and returning `None` on any failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `transition_close.py` emits at minimum a log/stderr line for each auto-derivation outcome (linked, skipped-ambiguous, skipped-no-commit, skipped-no-file-impact, write-failed) so operators can observe the feature's behavior.
evidence: Plan states 'best-effort — a failed resolution never blocks the close' but specifies no logging or user-facing feedback for any auto-derivation outcome.
ABSENCE: no mention of log.info/log.warning, stderr print, or metric emission when blame resolves, is ambiguous, or when `_write_link_event` raises.
findings.finding: Neither the auto-derivation outcome (skipped/linked/ambiguous) nor a `_write_link_event` failure is surfaced to the operator via a log message, stderr notice, or metric. An operator cannot determine why a `caused_by` link was or was not auto-derived after the fact.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py; src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — auto-link section
scenarios: User closes a bug expecting an auto-link; none appears in `rebar show`; no indication of why (no `file_impact`, no fixing commit, git error, write failure).
Ops team trying to audit why `caused_by` links are absent across a set of closed bugs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_write_link_event` (or its caller in `transition_close.py`) is idempotent — either by checking for an existing identical link event before writing, or by defining that duplicate events are deduplicated at read time by consumers.
evidence: Plan states 'Same direct-writer path is used for the explicit `--caused-by` override applied at close time' but does not mention deduplication or idempotency checks.
ABSENCE: no mention of 'check if link already exists before writing' or event-log deduplication semantics.
findings.finding: Re-running `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --caused-by <id>` (e.g., after a transient failure) will invoke `_write_link_event` again, potentially appending a duplicate `caused_by` link event to the store. The plan does not specify idempotency for `_write_link_event` under retry.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — _write_link_event direct-writer path; Approach section
scenarios: User runs close twice due to a transient error on the first attempt; `rebar show <bug>` displays two identical `caused_by` links.
At-least-once CLI retry automation appends duplicate link events to the store.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `--caused-by <id>` validates that the referenced ticket exists in the store before writing the link, and exits with a clear error message if not (mirroring validation done for other ID-reference parameters).
evidence: Plan says '`--caused-by <id>` always sets the link' (AC line 3) and 'applies the explicit `--caused-by <id>` override … via the `_write_link_event` direct-writer path' but specifies no existence check on the provided ID.
ABSENCE: no mention of 'ticket <id> not found → error', validation step, or user-facing error message for invalid IDs.
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` parameter accepts a ticket ID but the plan specifies no validation that the referenced ticket exists in the store. An invalid or mistyped ID would silently write a dangling `caused_by` link with no user feedback.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — `--caused-by <id>` parsing; transition_close.py — explicit override path
scenarios: User typos the ticket ID: `--caused-by PROJ-999` when PROJ-999 doesn't exist; link is written silently; provenance metrics now reference a ghost ticket.
User passes a valid-looking but wrong-project ID; no warning is issued.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE/cleanup path for `caused_by` link events: specify whether consumers must tolerate dangling references (and how), or whether a cascade/cleanup mechanism exists when a target ticket is removed.
evidence: Plan enumerates CREATE (blame.py + transition_close.py) but specifies no RETIRE path for `caused_by` link events.
Plan mentions 'consuming provenance metric (the registry/reader)' as a consumer but does not specify whether it tolerates a dangling reference (target ticket deleted/archived).
ABSENCE: no mention of cascading delete, tombstone, or consumer-side null-check for missing target ticket.
findings.finding: The lifecycle of `caused_by` link state written by `_write_link_event` has no RETIRE/cleanup path: if the culprit ticket referenced by the link is later deleted, renamed, or archived, the dangling link is never cleaned up or flagged, and no consumer tolerance for this absence is specified.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`; Approach section
scenarios: Culprit ticket is closed and purged from the store; `caused_by` link now references a non-existent ticket; provenance metric reader crashes or silently drops the edge.
Ticket is renamed/re-IDed; existing `caused_by` links become stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In `transition.py`, explicitly assert (or search the codebase for) all call sites of `_parse_flags` and confirm each is updated/compatible with the new 5-tuple return shape, acknowledging them in the plan.
evidence: Plan text: "`_parse_flags` returns an additional `caused_by` element (now a 5-tuple: `reason, force, force_close, close_class, caused_by`); `transition_cli` unpacks it"

Plan text: "mirrors ed13's `--class` threading, which already made `_parse_flags` return a tuple with `close_class`" — confirms this is at least a second tuple-shape change to the same function, but does not assert singleton caller count.
E3(d) requires: 'where the plan changes existing behavior, it acknowledges callers that depend on the old behavior.'
findings.finding: The plan changes `_parse_flags` from returning a 4-tuple to a 5-tuple but only names `transition_cli` as the consumer of that return value. Any other caller that positionally unpacks the 4-tuple (e.g., a test helper, a secondary CLI entry-point, or a wrapper added since ed13) would silently receive an IndexError or wrong-slot value at runtime. The plan does not state that `_parse_flags` has exactly one caller, nor enumerate all callers to confirm backward compatibility.
findings.location: `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` — "`_parse_flags` returns an additional `caused_by` element (now a 5-tuple: `reason, force, force_close, close_class, caused_by`); `transition_cli` unpacks it`"
scenarios: If any test helper or secondary CLI path in the existing codebase unpacks `_parse_flags()` as a 4-tuple, it receives an `IndexError` or silently reads a wrong slot after the change, breaking the caller without any signal in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: '`_parse_flags` is called only from `transition_cli` (verified by codebase search); no other callers exist that must be updated.' Alternatively, refactor to a named dataclass/attrs return to make tuple-arity changes non-breaking.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding integration test assertion) for `--caused-by <nonexistent-id>`: specify and test whether the command rejects the id before close, skips the link with a warning, or fails — whichever is the intended contract.
evidence: AC 3 only states: '`rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression --caused-by <id>` sets the link regardless of blame — test asserts presence.'
No AC or test describes what happens when `<id>` references a ticket that does not exist in the store — whether it raises, silently skips, or emits a warning.
ABSENCE: no 'invalid id', 'unknown ticket', or error-path AC for the `--caused-by` param.
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` CLI param (a new user-facing flow) has no test for the invalid/non-existent ticket id path: the ACs only assert that a valid `--caused-by <id>` sets the link, leaving the error-handling behavior unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 3 (`--caused-by <id>` always sets the link) and Files — `transition_close.py` / `transition.py`
scenarios: User typos a ticket id (`--caused-by EX-999` where EX-999 does not exist); the link is silently written to a non-existent target, or the close errors out — either is a user-visible behavior that is currently unspecified and untested.
A downstream consumer of the `caused_by` link iterates links and dereferences the target id, hitting a missing-ticket error.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`rebar transition <bug> ... --caused-by <missing-id>` where `<missing-id>` does not exist in the store emits a warning and skips the link (or errors before committing the close transition) — integration test asserts the defined behavior.' Choose one contract and encode it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Refactor `_referencing_commit_exists` (transition_close.py:32-57) into a shared helper that optionally returns the matched commit SHA (or extract a `_find_referencing_commit(accepted_ids, tracker, repo_root) -> str | None` sibling), so `blame.py`'s fixing-commit discovery reuses the same git-log scanning loop rather than re-implementing it.
evidence: transition_close.py:43-56 already runs `git log --format=%B%x00`, splits on NUL, calls `extract_ticket_refs(message)` per message, resolves each ref, and matches against accepted_ids — the same structural loop the plan describes for blame.py's fixing-commit discovery.

Plan section 'Fixing-commit discovery': 'reuse rebar._commands.verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(message) ... to parse rebar-ticket:/subject refs and match the bug id' — acknowledges reusing extract_ticket_refs but not the git-log loop itself.

The only difference is the return type: _referencing_commit_exists returns bool; blame.py needs the matched commit SHA. This is a refactor, not a rebuild — _referencing_commit_exists could be extended/extracted to also return the SHA.
findings.finding: The fixing-commit discovery loop in the proposed blame.py (walk git log, call extract_ticket_refs per message, match the bug id, return the matched SHA) overlaps substantially with the existing _referencing_commit_exists function in transition_close.py:32-57, which performs the same git log walk and extract_ticket_refs matching — differing only in returning a bool instead of the matched commit SHA. This is a partial NIH rebuild of an existing scanning loop rather than an extension of it.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py (proposed, fixing-commit discovery section) vs src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:32-57 (_referencing_commit_exists)
scenarios: If the git-log scanning logic in _referencing_commit_exists is updated (e.g. to handle worktrees, submodules, or a new trailer format), blame.py's copy would not be updated in sync — semantic drift between two parallel implementations of the same commit-matching scan.
Any bug in the NUL-delimited git log parsing (e.g. empty-message edge cases) would need to be fixed in two places.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_find_referencing_commit(accepted_ids, tracker, repo_root) -> str | None` next to `_referencing_commit_exists` in transition_close.py (or a shared `_commands/_commit_scan.py`). It returns the SHA of the first matched commit or None. `_referencing_commit_exists` becomes a one-liner wrapping it (`return _find_referencing_commit(...) is not None`). `blame.py` imports and calls `_find_referencing_commit` for its fixing-commit step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the variable name in the `_write_link_event` call inside `close_ticket`: use `tracker` (the actual local parameter name), not `tracker_dir`, to avoid a `NameError` at runtime.
evidence: Plan states: 'the auto-link therefore writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` **directly**'
Actual `close_ticket` parameter at transition_close.py:361 is named `tracker: str`, not `tracker_dir`; no `tracker_dir` variable exists in that scope.
ABSENCE: no aliasing of `tracker` to `tracker_dir` found in transition_close.py
findings.finding: The plan instructs calling `_write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` inside `close_ticket`, but the local variable in `close_ticket`'s scope is named `tracker` (not `tracker_dir`). Using `tracker_dir` verbatim would produce a `NameError` at runtime.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`, description of direct-writer path; `close_ticket` signature at transition_close.py:357-368
scenarios: Auto-derivation or `--caused-by` override executes inside `close_ticket` and calls `_write_link_event(..., tracker_dir)` — Python raises `NameError: name 'tracker_dir' is not defined`, crashing the close path.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `_write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` with `_write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker)` throughout the plan's description and implementation, matching `close_ticket`'s actual parameter name.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `blame.py`'s fixing-commit discovery re-uses `extract_ticket_refs` (for parsing) but must implement its own `git log` iteration to capture the matching commit hash — `_referencing_commit_exists` returns only `bool` and cannot be reused for hash retrieval.
evidence: Plan: 'reuse `rebar._commands.verify_commit.extract_ticket_refs(message)` … to parse `rebar-ticket:`/subject refs and match the bug id (the same trailer-resolution the close path's `_referencing_commit_exists`, transition_close.py:32, already relies on)'
`_referencing_commit_exists` at transition_close.py:32-57 returns `bool`, not the matched commit hash or SHA — there is no way to recover the fixing commit's hash from it.
The plan later needs the specific commit hash to compute `<fixing-commit>~1` for blame; this requires new iteration logic, not a call to `_referencing_commit_exists`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_referencing_commit_exists` (transition_close.py:32) is what the close path 'already relies on' to detect the fixing commit, and that the new `blame.py` will 'reuse `extract_ticket_refs`' to find the fixing commit's hash. However, `_referencing_commit_exists` only returns a `bool` and discards the matched commit hash — so any reuse of its logic in `blame.py` must be written from scratch (iterating `git log` and capturing the hash), not delegated to the existing function. The plan's phrasing 'reuse … the same … `_referencing_commit_exists`' implies structural reuse that does not exist.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`, 'Fixing-commit discovery' bullet
scenarios: Implementer reads 'reuse … `_referencing_commit_exists`' and attempts to call or adapt it, only to find it drops the commit hash — they must write the git-log iteration from scratch anyway, losing the planned reuse benefit and potentially introducing divergence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `_is_active_link(bug_id, culprit_id, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)` guard in `blame.py` (or in the `close_ticket` call site) before calling `_write_link_event`, so the auto-link is idempotent on retry.
evidence: _write_link_event docstring (graph/_links.py:124): 'Write a single LINK event to source_id's directory (no cycle check, no idempotency).'
Plan: 'the auto-link therefore writes via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir) directly'
Plan: 'Failure to resolve never blocks the close' — implies the caller can retry, but a retry re-fires the blame logic on an already-linked ticket
findings.finding: The `_write_link_event` direct-writer path the plan uses for auto-linking is explicitly non-idempotent (docstring: 'no cycle check, no idempotency'). If the post-close tail crashes after the STATUS commit but before the blame link write completes, and the user re-runs `rebar transition <bug> ... closed`, the bug is already closed so `transition_core` will likely reject the no-op or the blame logic re-fires anyway — and `_write_link_event` will write a second identical LINK event for the same `caused_by` relation. The plan does not specify an idempotency guard (e.g. `_is_active_link(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)` check before the `_write_link_event` call).
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — auto-link via `_write_link_event` / Approach section: 'the edge is written via `_write_link_event` directly'
scenarios: User runs `rebar transition BUG-1 in_progress closed --class regression`; close commits (STATUS event written), then blame link write crashes mid-commit; user re-runs the same command. Transition core may accept (optimistic concurrency already saw the close commit) or the tail re-fires. In either case, a second `_write_link_event` call on the same (bug, culprit, caused_by) triple writes a duplicate LINK event.
Automated pipeline retries a failed close command; blame auto-link fires twice, producing two `caused_by` LINK events for the same pair.
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling `_write_link_event(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)`, check `from rebar.graph._links import _is_active_link; if _is_active_link(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir): return` — the same idempotency guard `add_dependency` applies at line 266 of _links.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git blame` invocation format in `blame.py` (e.g. `git blame --line-porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <file>` emits one SHA per source line and is the correct form for the >50% line-count heuristic); document the chosen format and justify it against the alternatives.
evidence: Plan says 'blame the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fixing-commit>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact' but does not name a git blame invocation form or output format.

The majority threshold logic ('> 50% of the blamed lines across the file_impact files point to ONE commit') depends on counting one line-attribution per source line, which requires either --line-porcelain or careful stateful parsing of --porcelain output.
blame.py does not yet exist in the codebase (search confirms no src/rebar/metrics/blame.py); the format is therefore entirely unspecified.
git blame default output format has commit SHAs appearing only at hunk-start lines, not every line — naive line-counting of SHA appearances would undercount single-commit attribution.
findings.finding: The new `blame.py` resolver specifies the algorithm (>50% majority of blamed lines) but does not specify the `git blame` output format/invocation to use for line-to-commit attribution. The correct format choice is load-bearing: `git blame --porcelain` emits commit SHAs at hunk boundaries (requires stateful accumulation), `--line-porcelain` emits a SHA per line (simpler counting but verbose on large files), and the default output is human-readable (fragile to parse). An incorrect format silently miscounts attributions and produces wrong culprit derivations.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` new module
scenarios: Implementer uses `git blame --porcelain` and naively counts lines starting with a 40-char SHA (hunk boundaries only), undercounting the dominant commit's line share and returning None when a true majority exists.
Implementer uses default `git blame` output and parses the leading short SHA, getting truncated SHAs that collide across different commits (ambiguity).
Large file with many small hunks: --line-porcelain output is 12+ lines per source line, making the attribution parse correct but the invocation unexpectedly slow.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git blame --line-porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <file>` and count lines whose first token (the 40-char SHA header) matches the dominant commit, divided by total source lines in the output. This is unambiguous and directly supports per-line attribution counting. Note in the plan that the first commit edge case (`<fixing-commit>~1` fails when the fixing commit has no parent) must return None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `None`-return guard in `blame.py` for the case where the fixing commit has no parent (`git rev-parse <fixing-commit>~1` exits non-zero), consistent with the plan's 'failure to resolve never blocks the close' invariant.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'blame the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fixing-commit>~1)'
Plan lists four None-return cases: 'no file_impact, no fixing commit found, no >50% dominant commit, or no resolvable trailer' — the initial-commit edge case is not among them.
git resolves <sha>~1 to empty/error when sha has no parent (initial commit), causing subprocess to return non-zero and the resolver to either raise or silently fail depending on error handling.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the edge case where `<fixing-commit>~1` does not exist (i.e., the fixing commit is the repository's initial commit and has no parent). In this scenario, `git blame <fixing-commit>~1 -- <file>` will fail with a non-zero exit code, but the plan only lists four `None`-return cases (no `file_impact`, no fixing commit, no >50% dominant commit, no resolvable trailer) — the parentless-commit case is absent.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`, 'Blame target' paragraph
scenarios: A tiny repository (e.g., a test fixture or a freshly initialized repo where the first commit introduced the bug) where the culprit commit IS the initial commit — `git blame <sha>~1` fails and the resolver crashes instead of returning None cleanly.

findings.finding: `git blame` is a brand-new external CLI surface (zero uses in src/rebar) and the plan never specifies the output format flag. Default `git blame` output is human-readable column-based text; reliably extracting per-line commit SHAs requires `--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain`. The plan describes counting lines per commit SHA to compute the >50% majority but leaves the parsing approach entirely unspecified — a flag-level gap that will cause incorrect parsing or fragile string splitting in blame.py.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — new: single-culprit resolver / Blame target paragraph
scenarios: blame.py is implemented parsing default git blame output (human-readable, variable column widths) and misidentifies commit SHAs — the >50% majority logic silently produces wrong culprit links
A future git version changes default output formatting and silently breaks the parser
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <file>`: each output block starts with a 40-char SHA followed by line metadata, making per-line SHA extraction unambiguous and stable across git versions. Name this flag explicitly in the Files section for blame.py.

findings.finding: The Approach section claims 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative,' implying a confidence/provenance field is written on the link, but the Files section describes `_write_link_event` being called with no mention of a confidence argument, and no AC verifies that a confidence attribute appears on the resulting link.
findings.location: Approach section ('Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance'); Files / transition_close.py (_write_link_event call); all four ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Either add the confidence argument to the `_write_link_event` call description (e.g., `_write_link_event(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir, confidence='classified')`) and add an AC line like `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by").confidence=="classified"'`; or remove the provenance claim from the Approach if the field is not actually written.

findings.finding: The Approach section declares 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative,' but the LINK event payload written by `_write_link_event` is confirmed to be strictly `{target_id, relation}` with no confidence/provenance field. The plan never defines a schema extension to store this label, no new parameter is added to `_write_link_event`, and the `classified` vocabulary in `metrics/registry.py` applies to MetricValue/MetricSpec — not to link events. The referenced provenance label has no defined storage location or schema binding.
findings.location: Files section (blame.py) / Approach section — 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `metadata` dict to the LINK event payload for auto-derived links, e.g. `{'target_id': culprit_id, 'relation': 'caused_by', 'derived_by': 'blame', 'confidence': 'classified'}`, and update the `_write_link_event` call site in `transition_close.py` accordingly. Alternatively, if no storage is intended, remove the provenance claim from the Approach section to avoid an implementability gap.

findings.finding: The Approach section declares 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative,' implying a confidence-level field is set on the written link event, but no AC verifies that the auto-derived edge carries this confidence metadata rather than a higher/default level.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative'; Acceptance Criteria AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC #1's jq filter to: `jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .confidence=="classified")'`

findings.finding: The plan calls for `blame.py` to parse per-line commit SHAs from `git blame` output over `file_impact` files, but never specifies the output format flag. Default `git blame` produces human-readable annotated output that is fragile to parse (locale, config, line-count prefixes); the machine-parseable `--porcelain` (or `-p`) format is the established prior art for this use-case and is the only format that gives a guaranteed `<40-char-sha> <orig-line> <final-line> <count>` per-group header. Zero existing uses of `git blame` exist in `src/rebar`, so this is an unverified capability assertion — the plan asserts the SHA-counting algorithm works without pinning the format.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` / Approach section — 'Single-culprit threshold'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` and extract the 40-char SHA from lines matching `^[0-9a-f]{40} ` (the porcelain group header). This is the established prior art for programmatic blame parsing and is locale/config-independent.

findings.finding: `git blame` is introduced as a new external CLI surface (zero existing `git blame` invocations in `src/rebar`) but the plan never specifies the output format flag. Default `git blame` emits human-readable output with 8-character SHA prefixes, not full machine-parseable commit SHAs. Extracting per-line commit SHAs to count a dominant culprit requires `--porcelain` (full 40-char SHA per commit block, repeated for each line attributed to it) or `--line-porcelain` (a full header per line). Without the flag, the implementer must reverse-engineer the format, risking silent short-SHA collisions or broken parsing.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`, Blame target / Single-culprit threshold paragraphs
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git blame --line-porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <file>`: each line's block starts with a full 40-char SHA, making per-line commit attribution trivially parseable with a `line.split()[0]` on header lines (those not starting with a tab).

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'git blame <fixing-commit>~1' over file_impact paths will yield parseable, per-line commit SHAs, but does not specify the git blame output format. The default git blame output is human-readable prose, not machine-parseable; only --porcelain or --line-porcelain reliably yields commit SHAs per blamed line. No prior blame subprocess exists in src/rebar to establish a format convention.
findings.location: Files section: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — 'single-culprit resolver. Blames the pre-fix state'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blame.py file description: 'Uses `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` so each output block's first line is the 40-char commit SHA, which is parsed directly without regex over human-readable output.'

findings.finding: The plan introduces `git blame` as a new external CLI surface (zero existing `git blame` calls in src/rebar) but does not specify the output format flag. The default `git blame` output is human-readable prose; only `--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain` reliably yields stable, per-line commit SHA blocks machine-parseable enough to extract the culprit SHA without an ad-hoc regex. Without specifying the format, the implementer may choose the wrong format and produce a fragile parser that silently falls back to None on any moderately complex blame output (spaces in filenames, boundary commits, uncommitted-change markers), defeating the feature.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — 'single-culprit resolver. Blames the **pre-fix** state … over the bug's recorded `file_impact`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blame.py file description: 'Invokes `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` so each blamed hunk begins with the 40-char commit SHA on its own line, parsed directly without regex over human-readable output. Returns None if git exits non-zero (including when `<fixing-commit>~1` does not exist, e.g. a root commit).'
